evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go builds entry.ID from namespace/action via protocolActionContractID(...) while also maintaining separate hard-coded ContractID constants for the same actions.

goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go mixes two identity paths in one entry: generated ID for catalog binding and constant-based ID for metadata lookup, creating drift risk if either side changes independently.

goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go registers contracts by direct ContractID without namespace/action fields, while protocol_contracts.go uses namespace/action-derived IDs, indicating inconsistent contract boundary modeling across sibling modules.

Compatibility-alias APIs are duplicated as pass-through wrappers in multiple modules (for example `IsWifiEnabled` -> `IsWiFiEnabled`, `ConnectToWifi` -> `ConnectToWiFi` in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`, and `C2cCheckWith` -> `C2CCheckWith` plus `StartC2cCheck` aliasing in `goseg/main.go` and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`).

Large embedded runtime bags combine many unrelated capabilities (`workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`; `importerRuntime` embeds storage/upload/provision bags and orchestration ops in `goseg/importer/importer.go`; orchestration seam registry aggregates many ops structs in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Multiple flows repeatedly resolve defaults and nil fallbacks before doing domain work (`resolveWorkflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `resolveLogstreamRuntime` in `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go`, `normalizeDockerSubsystemRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`), increasing call depth for policy-free plumbing.
goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go and goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go define contract catalogs as inline literals in private slice-returning functions.

README.md describes quality gates and explicit per-contract assertions, but the contract-definition files do not expose a canonical, test-addressable manifest that automatically enforces complete coverage when new entries are added.
Because entries are hand-authored in multiple catalog functions, test completeness depends on manual synchronization rather than a single enumerated source for assertion generation.

Docker client close-and-error-join logic is duplicated with near-identical behavior in `closeRuntimeDockerClient` and `closeDockerSubsystemClient`, indicating repeated lifecycle policy code instead of one shared boundary.

`goseg/system/wifi_test.go` combines parser/unit concerns, runtime command seam behavior, C2C action routing, service lifecycle, and flow orchestration in one file, while `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` similarly mixes validation, shell command choreography, and matcher logic in a single large suite.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines many overlapping runtime-op structs and default builders (container/snapshot/health/startup/urbit/context/command/wireguard/netdata/minio/startup-load), creating fragmented seams with duplicated wiring patterns.

`goseg/structs/configs.go` exposes many per-section update methods with repeated nil/mutate checks while `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` separately defines another large field-mapping/update mechanism, showing split-but-overlapping mutation design.
HTTP upload handler forwards raw internal error text to clients (`failUploadRequest(..., err.Error(), ...)`) at multiple boundaries, including validation and pipeline failures.

Upload validation/runtime dependency failures are classified as `401 Unauthorized` because all `validateUploadRequest` errors are mapped to unauthorized, even when the cause is internal seam/runtime misconfiguration.

Long-running subsystem loops use inconsistent failure strategy: `check502Loop` logs and `continue`s on repeated operational errors while other loops (`CheckVersionLoopWithContext`, `OldLogsCleanerWithContext`) escalate after consecutive failures.

Transition/event bus saturation uses different error contracts in neighboring modules (exported sentinels in `docker/events` vs local wrapped error in subsystem events), reducing consistent caller handling with `errors.Is`.

findings.summary: Protocol contracts keep duplicated identity sources that can silently diverge.
evidence: protocol_contracts.go computes catalog entry ID from namespace+action (line 28) but metadata is keyed by separately declared ContractID constants (lines 17-19, 43, 52, 61).
The same contract concept is represented both as generated IDs and as hand-maintained string constants, so a rename or token change can desynchronize ID and metadata identity.
findings.suggestion: Use one canonical identity path: either define protocol entries by explicit ContractID constants only, or generate ContractID once and derive metadata key from that same value. Remove parallel hand-maintained ID constants or generated ID composition so only one source of truth remains.

findings.summary: Sibling contract catalogs encode boundaries differently, weakening uniform architecture contracts.
evidence: protocol_contracts.go populates Namespace and Action on entries (lines 29-30) and derives ID from that taxonomy.

startram_contracts.go inserts entries with only ID+Descriptor (lines 17-21) despite declaring family/scope taxonomy constants (lines 5-6), so taxonomy is not structurally encoded in the catalog entry.
This creates two binding styles inside the same contracts package, increasing coupling to per-file conventions instead of one package-level contract registration model.
findings.suggestion: Standardize registration shape across contract domains: introduce a shared constructor/API that requires the same boundary fields (or a shared typed taxonomy object) for both protocol and StarTram contracts, then migrate both files to that single pattern.

findings.summary: Legacy casing aliases create parallel APIs that add indirection without behavior
evidence: `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` defines multiple shim methods that only delegate (`IsWifiEnabled`, `ConstructWifiInfo`, `ListWifiSSIDs`, `ConnectToWifi`, `DisconnectWifi`) to WiFi-cased counterparts.
`goseg/main.go` keeps `defaultC2cRuntimeContext`, `defaultC2cRuntime`, and `C2cCheckWith` as pure delegations to C2C versions.
`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` duplicates startup callback slots (`StartC2CCheck` and alias `StartC2cCheck`) with fallback wiring in `Bootstrap`.
findings.suggestion: Keep one canonical public API per behavior (WiFi/C2C naming), move required legacy aliases into a small compatibility layer/file with explicit deprecation horizon, and remove duplicate fields/methods from primary runtime structs.

findings.summary: Embedded runtime capability bags hide true dependencies and blur boundaries
evidence: `workflowRuntime` embeds three orchestration ops structs plus workflow-specific callbacks, so transition helpers receive a broad bag rather than minimal contracts.
`importerProvisionRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps` and `RuntimeVolumeOps` while also carrying importer-specific callbacks, producing a mixed domain/infrastructure seam.

`runtimeSeamRegistry` in orchestration centralizes many unrelated ops groups (context/file/image/snapshot/urbit/wireguard/netdata/minio/volume) into one hub used as source for downstream runtime bags.
`startuporchestrator` defines very wide callback structs (`startupInitRuntime`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`) where many fields are unrelated to each specific startup phase.
findings.suggestion: Replace wide embedded bags with smaller phase-specific interfaces (for example per workflow step/import phase/startup phase), and construct those interfaces close to call sites so each operation depends only on required capabilities.

findings.summary: Repeated resolve/default wrappers increase call depth and obscure core logic
evidence: `operations_runtime.go` repeatedly resolves runtime inside helpers (`runTransitionLifecycle`, `dispatchUploadImport`, `emitWorkflowTransition`, `publishTransition`, `runTransitionedOperationWithRuntime`) even when a runtime is already provided.
`logs.go` has `*WithRuntime` + `*WithContext` + no-arg wrappers (`SysLogStreamer`, `DockerLogStreamer`, `DockerLogConnRemover`) that mostly normalize nil runtime/context before forwarding.
`subsystem_events.go` repeatedly calls `normalizeDockerSubsystemRuntime` and nil-context normalization across entrypoints and internal workers.
`broadcast/transitions.go` enforces variadic runtime + resolver for transition/mutation paths even though callers commonly pass default runtime directly.
findings.suggestion: Normalize runtime/context exactly once at subsystem boundaries, then pass explicit non-nil dependencies through internal functions; keep at most one public convenience wrapper per workflow and remove redundant intermediary variants.

findings.summary: StarTram contract IDs are hand-authored while action contracts use typed builders, creating boundary drift risk.
evidence: Protocol action contracts are assembled via typed namespace/action values and a constructor path (`protocolActionContract` + `protocolActionContractID`).
StarTram contract ID is declared directly as `"startram.errors.api-connection"` instead of being composed from contract family/scope primitives.
This creates two parallel contract-ID definition styles despite shared governance expectations in README.
findings.suggestion: Add a shared typed contract-ID constructor for non-action families (for example family/scope/name -> `ContractID`) in the contracts package, then migrate `APIConnectionError` and related definitions to that constructor so all families follow one contract-boundary pattern.

findings.summary: Contract catalog entries are not exposed as a single canonical manifest, making coverage checks manual and fragile.
evidence: Both catalog functions return private inline slices, so there is no explicit exported manifest tying all contract definitions to one enumerable source.
README requires explicit lifecycle and regression assertions for catalog changes, but this shape allows new literals to be added without a structural guarantee that tests enumerate them.
The enforcement strategy is policy-heavy (documentation/CI workflow) rather than code-structure-heavy in these contract declaration files.
findings.suggestion: Refactor catalog declarations into a single canonical manifest (or exported enumeration helper) consumed by both runtime registration and tests, then generate/assert coverage over that manifest so new contract entries cannot bypass test expansion.

findings.summary: Docker client close/error-aggregation policy is duplicated across lifecycle and subsystem packages.
evidence: `closeRuntimeDockerClient` and `closeDockerSubsystemClient` both perform the same sequence: nil guard, close, wrap operation text, assign or `errors.Join` onto caller error pointer.
Both helpers encode the same cross-cutting failure policy but live in different packages with different names, increasing drift risk when close/error behavior changes.
findings.suggestion: Extract one shared helper in a small internal Docker utility package (or shared lifecycle/orchestration seam) and route both call sites through it; keep operation label as an argument so behavior stays consistent while context remains specific.

findings.summary: Runtime seam design is over-fragmented, causing scattered ownership and repeated wiring patterns.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many narrowly split op structs plus multiple default builder functions, each wiring globals into similar callback fields.
Some capabilities are duplicated across op groups (for example copy-to-volume style callbacks in multiple structs), and runtime construction is spread across separate default factories.
`shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` introduces its own runtime factory/global runner indirection style, adding another parallel seam pattern instead of a consistent runtime composition model.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate runtime seams by domain boundary (for example: container lifecycle, config mutation, startram/network, startup) with fewer composed interfaces and one central constructor path; remove overlapping callback fields and standardize factory patterns across orchestration and shipworkflow runtimes.

findings.summary: Large test files mix unrelated concern layers, reducing suite navigability and change focus.
evidence: `wifi_test.go` includes low-level nmcli parsing tests, runtime command seam tests, adapter/action routing tests, and higher-level C2C flow behavior in one file.
`accesspoint_test.go` mixes config validation, process matching, command-formatting helpers, router start/stop orchestration, and extensive error-path command choreography in a single 900+ line unit.
`importer_test.go` is similarly large and multi-purpose, signaling repeated test-organization flattening where unit-level and orchestration-level concerns are collocated.
findings.suggestion: Split tests by behavior layer and module boundary (parser/runtime/service/flow or command-builder/orchestration/error-contract), keeping shared fixtures in small helper files; this preserves intent-focused files and reduces incidental coupling when one layer changes.

findings.summary: Config mutation logic is split between repetitive section mutators and schema-driven patching.
evidence: `SysConfig` exposes repeated `Update*Config` methods with identical validation and mutation scaffolding for each section.
`config_update_schema.go` separately maintains a large generic field registry/parser/apply pipeline for patch updates, overlapping the same domain of section mutation behavior.
Two mutation styles (manual section-specific mutators vs schema-driven patch application) create parallel design paths that increase cognitive load and maintenance inconsistency.
findings.suggestion: Unify around one mutation architecture: either route section updates through the schema/patch engine with typed wrappers, or generate section mutators from a single descriptor source so validation/apply logic is not duplicated across models.

findings.summary: Upload HTTP boundary leaks internal error details directly to client responses.
evidence: `HTTPUploadHandler` passes `err.Error()` and `pipelineResult.err.Error()` into `failUploadRequest` (lines ~830, ~842, ~859), making internal wrapped errors client-visible.
Validation paths can produce internal runtime/seam messages (e.g., token validation seam missing), which are then returned to clients unchanged.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a stable API error mapping layer (public error code/message + internal cause). Keep detailed wrapped causes in logs/telemetry only, and update tests to assert public contract rather than raw internal text.

findings.summary: Upload validation errors are over-bucketed as unauthorized, mixing auth failures with internal/runtime failures.
evidence: `validateUploadRequest` can return auth errors and internal dependency/runtime errors, but `HTTPUploadHandler` maps all validation failures to `http.StatusUnauthorized`.
Pipeline fallback maps unclassified failures to `http.StatusBadRequest`, including non-client causes, creating inconsistent failure semantics across adjacent paths.
findings.suggestion: Define typed/sentinel upload errors (authn, authz, validation, transient infra, internal misconfiguration) and centralize status mapping so auth errors map to 401/403, client input to 400, and internal/runtime faults to 5xx.

findings.summary: Background loops apply inconsistent escalation rules, causing unpredictable failure visibility.
evidence: `check502Loop` repeatedly logs operational errors (`GetShipStatusFn`, `LoadUrbitConfigFn`, HTTP polling) and continues indefinitely without a terminal error path.
`CheckVersionLoopWithContext` and `OldLogsCleanerWithContext` count consecutive failures and return explicit errors after threshold, surfacing sustained degradation.
findings.suggestion: Standardize loop error policy across subsystems: shared consecutive-failure budget, optional backoff, and explicit terminal/health signal behavior. Apply to 502 loop so persistent outages become observable and actionable.

findings.summary: Event bus saturation is represented by different non-interoperable error contracts across event modules.
evidence: `docker/events` exposes reusable sentinel errors (`ErrTransitionBusFull`, `ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`) enabling `errors.Is` checks.
`subsystem_events` uses a private `errEventBusFull` wrapped into formatted errors (`docker event bus saturated: %w`), which callers cannot consistently classify against shared event sentinels.
findings.suggestion: Unify on exported shared sentinel/type errors for bus backpressure and publish/cancel conditions; wrap with context but preserve a common comparable cause for all event producers/consumers.

findings.summary: Cross-module behavior is coordinated through mutable package-level default runtimes, creating hidden coupling and broad blast radius.
evidence: `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `runtimeRuntime`, `defaultRuntime`, and auth lifecycle globals hold process-wide mutable runtime state.
Public entry points rely on these defaults instead of requiring explicit runtime injection, so state changes in one area can affect unrelated callers.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit composition roots per subsystem and pass immutable runtime dependencies through constructors; keep package-level defaults only as thin compatibility adapters and freeze them after startup.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtime structs create high indirection cost with limited behavioral leverage.
evidence: `dockerContainerRuntimeInputs` aggregates dozens of function fields then remaps them into multiple templates and merges.
`clickRuntime` exposes many one-line forwarding methods and duplicates adapter surface.
`AccessPointRuntime` mixes config data and function seams, increasing API width and branchy defaulting.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into cohesive capability interfaces (file IO, container control, config snapshot, command exec), and replace method-per-command wrappers with small domain services that enforce policy once.

findings.summary: Error handling contracts are inconsistent across modules, with partial context loss and uneven message conventions.
evidence: `click/internal/runtime` uses `%v` in contextual errors (e.g., parse/create/execute paths), which breaks `errors.Is/As` chaining.
Other modules use `%w` wrapping and sentinel errors, so caller behavior differs depending on path.
Message casing/style is inconsistent (`Session not in client manager`, `Unrecognized container type`, lowercase forms elsewhere).
findings.suggestion: Adopt a subsystem-wide error contract: always wrap external-call failures with `%w`, normalize message style to lowercase sentence fragments, and define sentinel/typed errors for expected control-flow outcomes.

findings.summary: Accesspoint lifecycle and router modules mix too many responsibilities, reducing clarity and maintainability.
evidence: Router file includes shell safety validation, command formatting, interface bring-up/down, iptables mutation, process management, and preflight behavior.
Lifecycle coordinator handles dependency validation, state policy, config emission, and start/stop control with runtime flag branching.
findings.suggestion: Extract command-builder/sanitizer, network policy applier, and lifecycle policy into separate collaborators; keep coordinator limited to orchestration decisions and a narrow runtime interface.

findings.summary: Default production wiring paths are hard to validate because entry points instantiate concrete runtimes internally.
evidence: `LoadLlama` and `ContainerConfigForType` call `newDockerRuntime()` directly, bypassing explicit dependency injection at the entry boundary.
`accesspoint.Start` and broadcast loop defaults similarly rely on internal runtime construction and package globals.
This pattern makes unit tests exercise injected seams while leaving default integration wiring vulnerable to regressions.
findings.suggestion: Add black-box integration tests for default entry points and expose constructor-based entry APIs that accept full runtime dependencies, so default wiring is testable without global mutation.
